# The Architecture 2.0 Moonshot {#sec-moonshot}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The purpose of computing is insight, not numbers."*
>
> — Richard Hamming, *Numerical Methods for Scientists and Engineers* (1962)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Richard Hamming, a pioneer in computer science, famously emphasized that computing should yield insight rather than just raw data. For this chapter, the quote anchors the book's core premise. AI-assisted architecture should improve how architects formulate questions, test alternatives, explain mechanisms, and defend decisions, not merely expand the number of plausible candidates.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter introduces the Architecture 2.0 moonshot by turning a compact design request into a bounded AI-assisted computer architecture study. It shows why credible work must connect architectural intent, alternatives, executable artifacts, experiments, mechanism-level explanation, and a defensible decision.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What would it take for AI to help an architect formulate, explore, implement,
evaluate, explain, and defend a system design rather than merely generate a
plausible candidate?
:::

A compact prompt is not yet an architecture study. It becomes useful when the
architect expands it into a bounded question, meaningful alternatives,
checkable artifacts, experiments, a mechanism-level explanation, and a
recommendation whose limits another architect can challenge. AI may assist
each part of that work. It may also produce plausible fragments faster than a
team can connect them to a credible decision. The design loop makes the
movement among those parts explicit and inspectable. It is supporting
machinery, not the destination.

The moonshot therefore requires more than generation capacity. A system that
can propose a thousand accelerators but cannot test why they differ, explain
why candidates failed, or state what evidence would overturn the survivor has
not advanced architecture very far. It has enlarged the queue in front of the
same trust bottleneck. Cheap candidates become useful when a study can also
make unsupported candidates cheap to reject. A fast analytical model might,
for example, prune any accelerator that exceeds the thermal budget before the
team spends scarce simulator or physical-design time on it.

The book's running prompt makes that pressure concrete:

> **Lighthouse Prompt:** Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class LP mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives.

Computer architecture is the discipline that builds credible
hardware-software systems from workload intent, technology constraints,
software assumptions, physical limits, and evidence. It has never been only about which
tools you use; it is about how well you build, holding efficiency, safety,
robustness, and reliability to evidence rather than to assertion. Architecture
2.0 names the next step in that practice. AI can participate across
formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and defense,
but it remains grounded in architectural representations, tools, experiments,
and accountable judgment [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20].

The practical reason architecture needs this discipline is efficiency, but
efficiency no longer means a single
performance number. The classical quantitative tradition already treated
performance, cost, and power as coupled architectural questions
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Today, efficiency includes performance, energy,
power delivery, reliability, scalability, sustainability, cost, verification
burden, engineering effort, and time to credible evidence. Across that range,
the design problem couples hardware, software, tools, and deployment.
This multidimensional coupling is exactly what makes naive generation
insufficient. An AI-assisted study must state which constraints, tools,
experiments, and evidence can change its architectural claim. A represented
loop is one way to make that work executable rather than leave it implicit.

It helps to see how unusual this moment is. For roughly fifty years, processor
generations improved through a remarkably stable loop. Device scaling delivered
faster, cheaper, lower-power transistors on a predictable cadence; the
quantitative method turned design choices into measured comparisons; when
scaling slowed, microarchitecture, parallelism, and then domain specialization
kept the gains coming [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Fifty years of
frontier microprocessor data record that relay directly, with clock frequency
and typical power flattening in the mid-2000s while transistor budgets kept
climbing and core counts turned upward (@fig-microprocessor-trends).

```{python}
#| label: fig-microprocessor-trends
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Fifty years of one loop, and its era turns:** Frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021, transcribed from the public dataset maintained by Rupp, which compiles the collection of Horowitz, Labonte, Shacham, Olukotun, Hammond, and Batten. Clock frequency and typical power flatten in the mid-2000s as Dennard scaling ends [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon], logical core counts turn upward, and continued transistor growth flows into the specialization era Hennessy and Patterson call a new golden age [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Points are per-4-year frontier maxima from the dataset, not every processor, and era boundaries are approximate.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale line chart of frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021 with five series. Transistors per chip rise from a few thousand to tens of billions by 2021. Single-thread performance rises steeply until the mid-2000s and then slows. Clock frequency plateaus near 3 to 5 GHz and typical power near 100 to 500 W after about 2005, while logical core counts climb from one to the hundreds. Dashed vertical lines mark the approximate end of Dennard scaling around 2005 and the specialization turn around 2016, and a shaded band past 2025 marks a prospective fourth era of chiplet composition and AI-assisted design, an annotation of this book's argument rather than measured data."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

apply_style()

# Fifty-year microprocessor trend data; per-series frontier points
# (per-4-year-bucket maxima) transcribed from the public dataset
# github.com/karlrupp/microprocessor-trend-data (50yrs/*.dat, CC-BY 4.0),
# which compiles the Horowitz/Labonte/Shacham/Olukotun/Hammond/Batten
# collection through 2010 plus Rupp's additions through 2021. Values are
# dataset-transcribed, not recomputed; fractional years as in the dataset;
# transistors in thousands, single-thread performance in SpecINT x 1000.
# Era-boundary annotations (~2005 Dennard end, ~2016 specialization turn)
# are approximate: [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon;
# @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Accessed 2026-07-14.
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter1-micro-trend.csv
series = [
    {
        "label": "Transistors\n(thousands)",
        "color": COLORS["purple"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 6.1), (1982.3, 136), (1985.9, 274), (1989.5, 1210),
                   (1992.7, 3110), (1998.9, 15300), (2002.9, 221000),
                   (2007.0, 583000), (2009.7, 2.31e6), (2014.8, 5.7e6),
                   (2018.5, 2.11e7), (2021.9, 5.82e7)],
        "label_y": 5.82e7,
    },
    {
        "label": "Single-thread perf\n(SpecINT x 1000)",
        "color": COLORS["blue"],
        "points": [(1988.7, 83.5), (1994.8, 599), (1998.9, 3550),
                   (2002.4, 9390), (2007.0, 35200), (2010.3, 42000),
                   (2014.8, 66000), (2017.6, 83600), (2021.3, 105000)],
        "label_y": 1.05e5,
    },
    {
        "label": "Frequency (MHz)",
        "color": COLORS["orange"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 2.02), (1982.3, 6.1), (1985.9, 16.1),
                   (1988.7, 40.3), (1994.7, 297), (1997.5, 538),
                   (2002.4, 2250), (2004.9, 3650), (2007.7, 4660),
                   (2012.7, 3700), (2016.7, 4000), (2020.9, 3200)],
        "label_y": 3.2e3,
    },
    {
        "label": "Typical power (W)",
        "color": COLORS["red"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 0.922), (1982.3, 3.02), (1986.2, 3.02),
                   (1988.7, 3.96), (1993.0, 35.2), (1998.9, 90.6),
                   (2001.7, 98.2), (2003.4, 155), (2010.4, 168),
                   (2013.4, 300), (2018.5, 250), (2021.9, 500)],
        "label_y": 7.5e2,
    },
    {
        "label": "Logical cores",
        "color": COLORS["green"],
        "points": [(1971.9, 1), (1979.6, 1), (1985.9, 1), (1988.7, 1),
                   (1992.4, 1), (1995.4, 1), (1999.2, 1), (2006.0, 8),
                   (2010.2, 16), (2013.4, 240), (2016.5, 272), (2021.9, 208)],
        "label_y": 1.1e2,
    },
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 3.6))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.075, right=0.775, top=0.90, bottom=0.225)

ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(1970, 2032.5)
ax.set_ylim(0.4, 3e9)

for x in (2005, 2016):
    ax.axvline(x, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
               linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)

era_labels = [
    (1987.5, "Device-scaling era"),
    (2010.5, "Parallelism era"),
    (2019.6, "Specialization\nera"),
]
for x, text in era_labels:
    ax.text(x, 6.5e8, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=6.2,
            fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"])

# Prospective era band: annotation only, no data points. The band marks the
# book's argument, not a measurement.
ax.axvspan(2025, 2031, color=COLORS["note_fill"], alpha=0.55, zorder=0)
ax.axvline(2025, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
           linestyle=(0, (1.5, 2.5)), zorder=1)
ax.text(2028.8, 6.5e8, "Composition +\nAI-assisted\ndesign?", ha="center",
        va="center", fontsize=5.6, fontweight="bold",
        color=COLORS["workload_ink"], linespacing=1.2)
ax.text(2028.8, 3.2, "prospective\n(this book's\nargument)", ha="center",
        va="center", fontsize=5.0, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.2)

ax.text(2004.3, 4e7, "Dennard scaling\nends", ha="right", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)
ax.text(2016.4, 1.6e6, "specialization\nturn", ha="left", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)

for s in series:
    xs = [p[0] for p in s["points"]]
    ys = [p[1] for p in s["points"]]
    ax.plot(xs, ys, color=s["color"], linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=13, color=s["color"], zorder=3)
    ax.text(2024.9, s["label_y"], s["label"], ha="left", va="center",
            fontsize=6.0, fontweight="bold", color=s["color"],
            clip_on=False, linespacing=1.15)

ax.set_xticks([1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2020])
ax.set_yticks([1, 1e2, 1e4, 1e6, 1e8])
ax.set_yticklabels([r"$10^0$", r"$10^2$", r"$10^4$", r"$10^6$", r"$10^8$"])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Points are per-4-year frontier maxima transcribed from the public "
    "50-year dataset; era boundaries are approximate.",
    xywh=(0.075, 0.03, 0.70, 0.075),
    fontsize=5.6,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

That run of
specialization is often called a new golden age for computer architecture; its
quieter companion is a golden age of complexity, in which every design must
navigate an explosion of workloads, software interfaces, and physical
constraints. The artifacts changed every generation, while much of the work
that produced them remained human-orchestrated. Architects proposed, modeled,
measured, interpreted, and committed. AI can now assist a wider span of that
work, but its value and its bottlenecks depend on the task. Search may be
scarce in one study, simulator time in another, and mechanism-level
interpretation or trustworthy evidence in a third. @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales
develops that diagnosis in detail. This chapter asks what the broader practice
must make explicit before AI assistance improves architecture rather than only
its output rate.

The quantitative method gave architecture a shared vocabulary for performance,
cost, and power. Architecture 2.0 keeps that discipline. It also requires ways
to evaluate the representations, methods, tool paths, and experiments through
which AI participates, because weaknesses in those choices can change the
architectural conclusion.

Architectural intent now sits a long way from a software or hardware artifact
that is implemented, tested, and credible enough to use; explicit
representation is how a study covers that distance. AI can shorten parts of
that distance, but only when the work between intent and decision is
represented well enough to expose state, permitted actions, tool feedback,
evidence, and rejection. The moonshot is therefore a discipline for making
architecture work explicit enough that humans and AI systems can contribute
without hiding what the result means.

The purpose of this chapter is to make that shift concrete. We build the
chapter around a single design request, not because the request is already
solvable, but because it reveals the architecture work that any credible
answer would require.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can expand a compact architecture request into a bounded
AI-assisted study. That means you can:

- **Lineage:** Distinguish established, bounded uses of machine learning in architecture from broader AI participation across an architecture study.
- **Formulate:** Bound the lighthouse request in terms of workload, baseline, alternatives, constraints, and non-goals.
- **Explore and implement:** Identify meaningful alternatives and the executable artifacts and tool paths needed to compare them.
- **Evaluate and explain:** Specify experiments, counterevidence, and architectural mechanisms that could explain the results.
- **Defend:** State a bounded recommendation, its evidence limits, and the conditions that would reverse it.
- **Efficiency:** Treat efficiency as a multidimensional architecture claim rather than a single score.
:::

## Ask What AI Can Do for Architecture

The phrase "AI for architecture" can be read in a narrow but useful way. A
model can generate text, write scripts, summarize papers, or propose
configurations. The broader question is how the practice of computer
architecture changes when AI can assist the work that connects a question to a
decision.

This broader framing extends rather than replaces an established line of work.
Machine learning has already entered architecture through learned branch
predictors and prefetchers, surrogate models, reinforcement-learning
controllers, predictive design-space exploration, and autotuners. Chen,
Penney, and Jiménez synthesized that generation, organized component by
component, in *AI for Computer Architecture: Principles, Practice, and
Prospects*, the field's first consolidated map of that territory
[@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture]. Predictive design-space exploration
and tensor-program autotuning illustrate the bounded role
[@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM]. In much of that work,
learning was part of the artifact or a bounded method inside a human-specified
study. These roles remain useful.
The present shift is wider, and this book starts where that map ends. Its
subject is an engineering discipline for AI-assisted architecture studies, and
its unit of analysis is the study, not the component. AI may now help frame a problem, propose or edit
mechanisms, call tools, choose experiments, adapt to simulator feedback,
interpret results, and influence which candidate reaches review. Machine
learning is no longer only inside the design. It can also participate in
producing the claim about the design.

Architecture work has always moved through repeated analysis and revision.
Architects frame a problem, choose abstractions, construct models or
simulators, select workloads, explore alternatives, evaluate results, reject
weak candidates, and revise assumptions. Consider an architect weighing a
larger L2 cache. The study asks whether it helps a stated workload mix without
hurting energy, uses gem5, a cycle-level architecture simulator, or FireSim, an
FPGA-accelerated full-system RTL simulation platform, sweeps cache sizes and
policies, and reads the resulting miss rates, performance, and energy
estimates. The architect must then explain whether locality, contention, or
latency hiding caused the result and state whether the evidence supports a
cache change. Simulators, compilers, profilers, synthesis tools, spreadsheets,
dashboards, and design reviews all mediate that judgment.

AI systems earn a place in the study when they can contribute across it.
They may generate candidates, edit artifacts, call tools, summarize evidence,
predict outcomes, search design spaces, critique assumptions, or coordinate
subtasks. Participation is credible only when the execution path exposes what
the system can see, what it can change, how feedback is obtained, what evidence
is trusted, and what can reject the result.

AI systems are participants in the study, not the study itself. A method may
propose a candidate, estimate an outcome, search alternatives, critique an
assumption, or check a result. A tool is useful when its actions,
observations, costs, and failure modes are visible. Architects or accountable
engineering teams still set the intent, choose the abstraction and fidelity,
interpret the architectural mechanisms, decide what evidence is adequate, and
define which decisions may be automated. Later chapters separate these jobs
and specify the interfaces they require.

::: {.column-margin}
**On the term "participant":** It is often singular in the prose and figures, but names an AI-assisted system that a real implementation may realize as one generative method, a pipeline of methods, or several specialized tools. The Architecture 2.0 question is still what each participant may see, change, test, and return for an architect-owned decision.
:::

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Accountable Commitment"}
AI methods can propose and test candidates, and declared automated checks can
reject routine failures. The architect or accountable engineering team sets
the objective, the evidence standard, and the limits on automated approval.
Responsibility for a consequential architecture claim does not pass to the
method that produced it.
:::

Those parts together define the discipline used throughout the book.

> **Architecture 2.0.** Architecture 2.0 is the engineering discipline of
> using AI, grounded in architectural representations, tools, and experiments,
> to formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer
> architecture decisions.

AI participation is the premise, not the achievement. The preface's division
of labor carries through unchanged, with the bounded architecture study as the
unit of work and the architectural claim as the unit of review. The
representations, tool interfaces, evidence records, rejection checks, and
decision rights introduced later make that work inspectable without
substituting for the architectural contribution.

It helps to place this wider form of AI participation on the familiar
automation ladder that @fig-automation-ladder traces across five eras. The
dates are approximate; the point is the widening unit of intent and the widening
artifact produced.

1. **1980s--1990s (Logic Synthesis):** Logic synthesis turns Boolean and RTL intent into gate-level structure, governed by libraries, physical constraints, and timing feedback [@DeMicheli1994SynthesisOptimization].
2. **1990s--2000s (High-Level Synthesis):** High-level synthesis turns algorithmic behavior into RTL candidates, offloading the tactical microarchitecture search from the human to the tool [@CoussyMorawiec2008HighLevelSynthesis].
3. **2000s--2010s (Domain-Specific Synthesis):** Building on deductive program synthesis from the 1970s, domain-specific synthesis turns mathematical kernels and DSL intent into executable code and specialized hardware/software artifacts [@GulwaniPolozovSingh2017ProgramSynthesis].
4. **2010s--2020s (Agile Hardware Generation):** Agile hardware generation turns parameterized templates (e.g., Chisel, a Scala-embedded hardware construction language, and its FIRRTL intermediate representation) into bespoke SoC instances, enabling rapid specialization through reusable IP libraries.
5. **2020s onward (AI-Assisted Architecture):** AI can help connect workload intent, software contracts, architectural alternatives, executable tools, experiments, and physical constraints inside a bounded study. The goal is an evidence-backed finding or decision, not a claim that the entire path is automated.

![**Five rungs of design automation widen the unit of intent:** Each rung accepts a larger statement of intent and produces a larger artifact, from Boolean and RTL descriptions yielding gate-level structure to the workload intent of a bounded study yielding an evidence-backed finding or decision. Era spans are approximate and follow the literature cited above.](images/F2b-automation-ladder-timeline){#fig-automation-ladder width="100%" fig-alt="Staircase diagram of five design-automation rungs rising from lower left to upper right, each box wider than the one below. From bottom to top: logic synthesis (1980s to 1990s) turning Boolean and RTL intent into gate-level structure, high-level synthesis (1990s to 2000s) turning algorithmic behavior into RTL candidates, domain-specific synthesis (2000s to 2010s) turning kernel and DSL intent into specialized code and hardware, agile hardware generation (2010s to 2020s) turning parameterized templates into bespoke SoC instances, and an emphasized final rung for AI-assisted architecture (2020s onward) turning workload intent into an evidence-backed finding or decision. A horizontal arrow marks approximate time order and a vertical arrow marks the widening unit of intent and artifact."}

The earlier rungs have mature forms of automation and supply important tools
and artifacts for the broader practice. Each rung also earned adoption only
when its output became independently checkable; designers trusted synthesized
netlists once static timing analysis and equivalence checking could reject bad
ones. Architecture 2.0 asks how AI can help an architect connect the rungs
while preserving the meaning and limits of the result.

The bounded architecture study is the object this book will develop. Its design
loop is the repeated movement from intent to bounded action, feedback,
evidence, rejection, revision, and architect-owned commitment. The loop matters
because it exposes how the study was carried out.

The book's audiences were set out in the preface; the framework itself earns
its keep in three uses:

- **Researcher:** Describe an AI-for-architecture paper by naming the
  architecture question, the role AI played, the artifact or finding produced,
  and the evidence and limits behind it.
- **Tool builder:** Ask whether a harness records enough state for another
  method or team to learn from it.
- **Author or reviewer:** Ask what would reject the claim, why the measured
  mechanism supports it, and which decision it can justify.

Later chapters turn those needs into reusable reports and review checks.

## From Architecture 1.0 to Architecture 2.0 {#sec-from-architecture-10-to-architecture-20}

Architecture 1.0 and Architecture 2.0 distinguish modes of participation, not
an obsolete practice from a superior one. Architecture 1.0 is shorthand for
the familiar practice of human-orchestrated design. The architect defines the
problem, chooses models, uses tools, interprets feedback, and decides what to
build. This practice is the foundation on which the field stands.

Architecture 2.0 allows AI to participate across more of the study. The
architect still owns intent, constraints, abstraction, evidence standards,
mechanism-level interpretation, and accountability. Broader AI participation
also creates a practical obligation. The study must represent enough state,
permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, and rejection for another
architect to understand what happened and what the result can support.

The difference can be seen in a familiar design-space exploration. In
human-orchestrated practice, an architect might manually script a simulator sweep over
cache sizes, associativities, and replacement policies, then inspect the
results. In an AI-assisted study, a
method proposes candidates, a surrogate[^fn-ml-surrogate-model] estimates outcomes, a simulator
evaluates selected points, a critic flags invalid assumptions, and a human
decides whether the evidence is strong enough. The contribution may be a cache
artifact, an architectural finding, a reusable method or dataset, or a bounded
decision. The represented loop makes that contribution inspectable. It does
not determine what the contribution must be.

[^fn-ml-surrogate-model]: A surrogate model is a cheap, data-driven stand-in for an expensive simulator. In a study it lets a method screen many candidates quickly, but its score is only a proxy. A promising candidate still has to be escalated to higher-fidelity evidence before commitment.

@fig-design-loop-breaks captures the operational difference. The left loop is
the familiar human-carried practice, where architectural judgment coordinates
intent, models, candidates, tool runs, and expert review. The right loop does
not remove that judgment. It represents enough state, permitted actions, tool
feedback, evidence, and rejection for bounded AI methods to participate
without becoming an uninspectable prompt-to-chip shortcut.

![**AI participation changes what an architecture study must expose:** A represented execution loop makes state, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, and rejection explicit rather than leaving them in human-carried practice.](images/F2a-design-loop-breaks){#fig-design-loop-breaks width="100%" fig-alt="Two side-by-side loop diagrams compare a human-orchestrated architecture study with an AI-assisted study whose execution exposes state, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, and rejection."}

Inspectability is why the book represents the execution and analysis loop at
all. A participant, whether one method or a
coordinated set of methods, is not the whole study. Its actions must connect to
explicit representations, tool interfaces, evidence records, and accountable
decision points. Those connections let another architect inspect how an
artifact or finding was produced and whether it supports the proposed claim.

::: {#pri-design-the-loop .callout-design-principle title="Design the AI-Assisted Study, Not Only the Generator"}
A generated artifact, candidate configuration, or plausible answer is not
enough on its own. The durable contribution may be an architecture artifact,
insight, method, dataset, environment, or decision. Design the study so another
architect can inspect its question, alternatives, state, actions, tool
feedback, evidence, explanation, and decision before accepting the stronger
architecture claim.
:::

A useful AI-assisted system should not merely know how to call a simulator,
propose RTL, or summarize papers. It should carry the habits that make systems
engineering credible. It should suspect the bottleneck, account for memory
movement and physical limits, compare tradeoffs numerically, preserve
provenance, expose failure modes, and ask what evidence is strong enough for
the next commitment. Those habits do not appear automatically because a model
is capable. They have to be designed into the study and its tool path.

## The Architecture Moonshot

Before returning to the concrete prompt as an architecture object, we must fix two terms: *architecture* and *architect*. In
this book, architecture does not mean only microarchitecture, a block diagram,
or a chip artifact.

> **Architecture.** Architecture is the hardware-software contract and system
> organization that turn workload intent and technology constraints into a
> defensible system design. It includes instruction set architecture (ISA) and microarchitecture, memory and
> interconnect, accelerators and chiplets, compiler/runtime interfaces,
> physical-design constraints, verification, deployment, and the evidence used
> to justify choices.

> **Architect.** The architect is the accountable decision role that owns the
> intent, frames the problem, chooses the abstractions, sets the evidence
> standard, defines what can reject a candidate, and remains answerable for the
> committed design. One person, an engineering team, or several authorized
> review roles may perform that work, but the study must make their decision
> rights and final accountability visible. AI methods contribute inside the
> study. The architect owns the synthesis and the architecture claim.

With those terms fixed, we can read the lighthouse prompt as more than a
compact design request. It asks for a compute subsystem serving real-time
mobile extended reality (XR), under a thermal-design-power (TDP) budget in a
low-power (LP) mobile process class, and it asks for a report rather than a
chip. Its purpose here is diagnostic. The request is deliberately beyond
present end-to-end capability so that it exposes what a credible study would
have to carry.

The canonical prompt leaves more specific accelerator, memory, and integration
choices open so that one sentence can travel through the book unchanged.

The sentence introduced at the start of the chapter is quoted fragment by
fragment throughout the book, and @fig-moonshot-prompt keeps it visible as an
object of analysis. The top panel restates the prompt; the middle panel names the
architectural obligations embedded in that request; and the bottom panel shows
the execution and analysis turn needed before an AI system's answer deserves
architectural trust. The study must represent the task, act through bounded
tools and methods, gather evidence, reject weak outputs, explain the result,
decide what to commit, and revise the next turn. The point is to expose the
work a credible answer requires.

![**The lighthouse prompt expands into an architecture study:** It surfaces the architecture obligations and the execution machinery needed to formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend a credible answer.](images/F1-moonshot-stack){#fig-moonshot-prompt width="92%" fig-alt="Stacked diagram with the full lighthouse prompt at the top, architecture obligations in the middle, and the represented execution and analysis requirements needed before a result can be trusted at the bottom."}

> **Lighthouse prompt.** The lighthouse prompt is the compact design request
> above, quoted unchanged throughout the book and answerable only with a
> design-space report carrying evidence and rejected alternatives.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="Read the Prompt as an AI-Assisted Architecture Study"}
**Context.** The lighthouse prompt is not a miniature product specification. It
is a compact way to expose the work a credible AI-assisted architecture study
would have to carry.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** "64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem" sets the
ISA/ABI and software contract the method must respect. "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload" sets
the workload scope the AI method must optimize. "vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block" sets the
compute organization bounds for the method's action space. "3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class LP mobile
process" sets the physical-design envelope that can reject a generated
proposal, and "design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives"
sets the evidence standard the study owes the architect. Those fragments force
the study to carry memory, data movement, compiler, runtime, reliability, and
verification state rather than treating the prompt as a simple hardware
generation task.

**Takeaway.** Treat the prompt as a recurring stress test. A proposed study has
not yet answered it if it cannot expose the question, alternatives, artifacts,
experiments, explanation, recommendation, evidence limits, and decision owner.
:::

The book keeps the mobile XR prompt as its single lighthouse so that the same
request can accumulate detail rather than compete with a second running
example. A datacenter problem, such as mapping a 10T-parameter
mixture-of-experts[^fn-ml-mixture-of-experts] model across a large accelerator
fabric, would expose different details: compiler mappings, collective traffic,
tail latency, fault recovery, and software ecosystem compatibility. Those
details change the engineering, but not the need to expose hidden obligations
before trusting an answer.

[^fn-ml-mixture-of-experts]: A mixture-of-experts (MoE) model selectively routes each input to a small, specialized subset of its neural-network weights, reducing compute cost per token while keeping a large total parameter count. That routing sparsity is what makes the datacenter prompt an architecture problem, because the study must represent how the tensor compiler maps experts to devices and how the interconnect survives the resulting all-to-all traffic.

The datacenter prompt is deliberately extreme, just as the mobile prompt is at
a smaller scale. No architect, and no model, can turn that
sentence into a verified subsystem today, and that is the point. Pushing the
request past what is currently feasible is a forcing function. It exposes the
hidden architectural state, evidence, and decisions a credible study would
have to handle, rather than letting a plausible-sounding answer conceal them.

An extreme request has a way of becoming routine. Provisioning compute once
meant hardware procurement, capital planning, and long lead times; today a
developer can spin up a cloud instance through a console or API.
Writing fast machine code was once a craft only experts practiced by hand, and
the optimizing compiler turned a stated high-level intent into code that beats
them. Each sounded absurd before the engineered chain beneath it existed, and
each became ordinary once that chain was built.[^fn-logistics-amazon] The wager
of this book is that architecture can begin to build a comparably disciplined
chain around a prompt like this one. The work lies in engineering the representations, tools,
experiments, and feedback path that make an AI-assisted answer credible, not in
waiting for a bigger model.

[^fn-logistics-amazon]: The pattern is not unique to computing. Amazon-style
retail logistics turned ordering a book from a trip to a store into a single
click that sets off an orchestrated chain of inventory, robotics, packing,
routing, and delivery. What made it ordinary was engineering every step to be
reliable and composable, a related move this book asks of an AI-assisted
architecture study.

With that concrete prompt in view, the word *moonshot* can be used precisely.
The term should not mean a prediction that the field can already automate
architecture end to end. X, the Moonshot Factory, frames a moonshot as the
intersection of a huge problem, a radical solution, and a breakthrough
technology that makes the solution plausible enough to pursue
[@XCompanyMoonshotBlueprint]. This book adapts that structure to computer
architecture.

Use the moonshot analogy only to name a field-building requirement. A hard
target becomes useful when it defines shared tasks, representations,
instruments, feedback, tests, and evidence standards. Apollo, the Human Genome Project, DARPA's Grand
Challenge, and AlphaFold matter here as examples of targets that created shared
tasks, instruments, datasets, tests, and evidence standards, not as a claim that
architecture should imitate their politics, budgets, or technical domains
[@NASA2008Apollo11; @NHGRI2025HumanGenomeProject; @DARPA2014GrandChallenge; @JumperEtAl2021AlphaFold]. The common pattern is not that a moonshot is
large or fashionable. It is that the target organizes a community around a hard
problem, a different way of working, and an enabling technical shift.

There is a sharper lesson here than the analogy first suggests. Each example
is remembered as a singular achievement, a flag on the Moon, a finished genome,
a solved protein structure. But the durable contribution was rarely the single
result. It was the shared task, the instruments, the methods, and the evidence
standards that turned an exceptional effort into a process others could run.
Architecture 2.0 takes that stance. The goal is to build the shared
representations, tools, methods, experiments, and evidence practices through
which credible architecture results can be produced, checked, and reproduced
rather than admired as isolated showcases. Computer architecture has done
exactly this before, more than once.

The Mead and Conway VLSI design methodology turned chip design into a
structured, reusable discipline with shared abstractions and design rules
[@MeadConway1980VLSI], and the reduced-instruction-set program
reshaped the hardware/software contract around quantitative evidence
[@PattersonDitzel1980RISC]. Mead and Conway's durable contribution also joined
those abstractions and rules to tool and fabrication access, pedagogy, and a
community able to practice the method. Both programs changed how the field
worked, not just the artifact it produced. Architecture 2.0 should aspire to
that kind of contribution. Whether it earns a place in that lineage will depend
on whether its abstractions, instruments, methods, and evidence practices
become useful to people beyond the projects that introduced them.

> **Architecture moonshot.** An architecture moonshot is an aspirational target
> at the intersection of three conditions: a grand architecture challenge that
> ordinary ad hoc coordination cannot evaluate credibly enough, a radical
> practice target that changes how AI-assisted architecture work is formulated,
> explored, implemented, evaluated, explained, and defended, and an enabling
> AI/data/tool breakthrough that makes the target technically plausible enough
> to study without pretending it is solved.

That same pattern carries into the architecture vocabulary used here, as @fig-moonshot-venn shows.
The phrase is deliberately about the field's capacity, not about dismissing classical
architecture practice. Classical architecture already has models, measurements,
review, and signoff discipline. The moonshot is to make enough of that practice
explicit, tool-connected, and evidence-bearing that AI methods can participate
without hiding what their output means or what would reject it.

![**A moonshot needs three conditions at once:** An architecture moonshot sits at the intersection of a grand architecture challenge, a radical change in AI-assisted architecture practice, and an enabling AI/data/tool breakthrough that makes the target plausible enough to study.](images/F1c-moonshot-venn){#fig-moonshot-venn width="100%" fig-alt="Three-circle Venn diagram showing an architecture moonshot at the intersection of a grand challenge, a radical practice target, and an enabling breakthrough."}

This architecture-specific moonshot is worth naming because the pressure is real but the
solution is not yet settled. The grand challenge is that hardware/software
systems now span workloads, software stacks, ISAs, microarchitecture,
accelerators, memory systems, Electronic Design Automation (EDA), physical constraints, verification, and
deployment. The radical target is not to automate architects away. It is to
connect representations, methods, and tools that explore these choices,
preserve evidence, explain mechanisms, record failures, and make clear what may stop a candidate across
ISA, microarchitecture, compiler, RTL, physical-design, and deployment
boundaries. The enabling shift is the arrival of AI methods, architecture
datasets, executable environments, and tool interfaces that make pieces of those
studies plausible enough to conduct. Publicly reported examples already include reinforcement
learning[^fn-rl-reinforcement-learning] systems for physical-design exploration and learned circuit-generation
loops with silicon evidence
[@Synopsys2023DSOai; @RoyEtAl2021PrefixRL]. @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack returns to those examples
as loop-pattern cases. These examples show bounded methods and tool paths with evidence,
not end-to-end architecture autonomy. AgentDSE, a recent LLM-agent system for
simulator-in-the-loop design-space exploration, adds the same lesson in a DSE
setting. It delegates pieces of architectural reasoning while simulator feedback
keeps the search grounded [@WangEtAl2026AgentDSE]. The moonshot is not that
these pieces exist. It is a discipline that can connect them to architecture
questions, experiments, explanations, and decisions that a human can still
review and own.

[^fn-rl-reinforcement-learning]: Reinforcement learning is a machine-learning method in which an agent learns to choose actions that maximize a cumulative reward signal from its environment, often used to navigate complex design spaces [@SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. In an AI-assisted architecture study, it is one bounded search method, powerful for exploration but still owing the same evidence and checks as any other proposal source.

Growth is not the same as progress, and a fast generator is not a field.
Computer architecture has learned this lesson repeatedly. SPEC-style benchmarks
made performance claims more comparable because they carried workloads, run
rules, and reporting discipline. Shared simulators such as gem5 made
architecture experiments easier to inspect across labs. FPGA-accelerated
simulation platforms such as FireSim, community benchmark suites such as MLPerf,
artifact-evaluation practices, and open tool flows each pushed the same lesson
in a different setting. A community advances when its results travel with enough
context that someone else can understand, rerun, challenge, or safely build on
them [@SPEC2017CPU; @BinkertEtAl2011Gem5; @KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim;
@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf].

The broader run of AI moonshots makes the same point from the other direction. AlphaGo [@SilverEtAl2016AlphaGo], AlphaFold [@JumperEtAl2021AlphaFold], GraphCast [@LamEtAl2023GraphCast], Med-PaLM [@SinghalEtAl2023MedPaLM], and AlphaProteo [@ZambaldiEtAl2024AlphaProteo] each cleared a bar the field thought was years away, and each is remembered as a singular result more than as a repeatable practice. A flagship is not yet a discipline. You cannot produce results like these on demand the way a mature field turns out bridges or compilers. The moonshot that matters next is not a bigger model. It is the engineering layer of measurement, standards, shared artifacts, and trained people that makes results reproducible, comparable, and composable. AI-assisted architecture does not yet share a widely adopted version of that layer.

Our own field has a flagship of exactly this kind, and it is this book's second running example. Where the lighthouse is a synthetic ideal, this one is real. AlphaChip is a genuine landmark, a learning method that performs macro placement on production netlists, a bounded physical-design task [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. Google said it was used in designing its tensor processing units. It is also one of the field's most public evidence disputes [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @Markov2023FalseDawn], because the public record around it did not fully expose the evidence trail, agreed baselines, and shared authority to say no. As of July 2026, the public record still does not provide an independent reproduction of the strongest deployment and pretraining claim. A celebrated achievement became a stress test for the field infrastructure this chapter argues for.

AlphaChip returns through the book as a war story at key checkpoints, and the story accumulates toward a test. @sec-running-the-loop runs the book's review instrument backward over the public record and shows where the dispute was always going to break out. The war story is not a takedown. AlphaChip is one of the most visible architecture results for AI-designed hardware, and we use it as a stress test because it is influential, reported as deployed, and genuinely contested, not because it is uniquely suspect. Where the lighthouse shows the architecture we could have, the war story shows the architecture we have. The contrast runs through the book.

::: {.callout-war-story title="A Flagship, Not Yet a Discipline"}
**The claim.** In 2021, a Google team reported a reinforcement-learning method that generates a chip's macro placement in under six hours, at quality comparable to or better than human experts on key power, performance, and area metrics [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. It was named AlphaChip in a 2024 *Nature* addendum [@GoldieEtAl2024AlphaChipAddendum] and, Google later said, used across several TPU generations [@GoogleDeepMind2024AlphaChip]. The production setting and reduction from weeks of expert work to hours suggested that a learned method might reach the frontier of silicon design, not only assist at its edges.

**The gap.** Independent groups could not reproduce the reported advantage. A 2023 reassessment with open code and shared test cases found that a strong simulated-annealing baseline matched or beat the method's proxy cost on most modern test cases, that commercial and analytical placers often produced substantially lower routed wirelength, and that the proxy correlated only weakly with metrics that decide a real chip [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. The original authors replied that the critics skipped central pretraining, did not train to convergence, used far less compute, and tested on designs they considered unrepresentative [@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed]. The proprietary designs used for pretraining were not released, for the legitimate reason that they are Google's own chips. Google later released code and a pretrained checkpoint, but not those inputs. In September 2024 *Nature* removed an earlier editor's note, said the concerns were resolved to its satisfaction, and published a clarifying addendum. That closed the journal's process. It did not produce an independent public reproduction of the strongest pretraining and deployment claims, and the adjudication has since changed venues rather than ended. It has also cut in both directions. The reassessment's authors reconfirmed their conclusions in a 2026 journal update of their study [@ChengEtAl2026UpdatedAssessment], while as of July 2026 the CACM version of a separate prominent critique [@Markov2023FalseDawn] carries an ACM expression of concern recording an ongoing investigation into its content and disclosure. An expression of concern marks an open inquiry, not a finding, so that question too remains unsettled rather than decided.

**The lesson.** The public record remains contested. Google reports deployment across TPU generations, while no independent, apples-to-apples benchmark has settled whether the full method beats strong classical baselines under the same conditions. This book takes no side on who is right. The dispute shows why AI-assisted architecture needs shared ways to release, replay, and adjudicate consequential claims.
:::

Returning to the lighthouse prompt, each fragment sets a specific constraint. XRBench, a benchmark suite for mobile XR workloads, gives the prompt a workload anchor rather than a vague application
label [@KwonEtAl2023XRBench]. End-to-end XR systems research has shown how
tightly the stages of the full perception-to-display pipeline couple, which is
what makes mobile XR a demanding architecture target rather than a single
kernel to optimize [@HuzaifaEtAl2021ILLIXR]. The node is
intentionally stated as a class rather than as a named foundry PDK (process design kit); current
mobile SoCs are publicly
described in 3 nm-class technology, but a credible architecture study
must still state which process, libraries, voltage assumptions, and signoff
path it actually uses [@Apple2024A18Pro; @Apple2024M4].

The familiar foundation-model[^fn-ml-foundation-model] stack offers one limited
analogy for the prompt. Architecture has heterogeneous inputs, including
workload traces, specifications, RTL or IP blocks, simulator configurations,
process assumptions, verification logs, papers, and prior designs. It also has
many outputs, including ISA proposals, microarchitecture sketches,
accelerator or SoC partitioning choices, RTL and testbench fragments,
design-space reports, verification packages, and deployment decisions. The
analogy stops at that many-to-many shape. The center is not one foundation
model or a current capability claim. It is a bounded architecture study
supported by representations, tools, methods, experiments, and judgment.

![**The foundation-model analogy has a strict limit:** Architecture has heterogeneous inputs and outputs, but its center is a bounded study connected to artifacts, tools, experiments, evidence, and judgment rather than a prompt-to-chip model.](images/F1a-foundation-wall-stack){#fig-foundation-wall-stack width="100%" fig-alt="Two-panel diagram contrasting a generic foundation-model stack with an AI-assisted architecture study that connects heterogeneous inputs, tools, evidence, and architecture deliverables."}

@fig-foundation-wall-stack translates that limited analogy. For the lighthouse
prompt, one study turn might ingest an XRBench trace slice and a
software pipeline description, generate vector-width, local-memory, and
CPU/accelerator partition candidates, run a cheap performance and power proxy,
reject candidates that miss the latency or 3 W envelope, and preserve the
rejected alternatives as evidence for the next turn. A stronger-evidence turn
could replace some proxy evidence with simulator runs, compiler output, RTL
fragments, or physical-design feedback. The foundation-model analogy is useful
only when it points beyond the model to that maintained architecture state,
tool path, and evidence.

[^fn-ml-foundation-model]: A foundation model is a large-scale AI model trained on a vast quantity of data that can be adapted to a wide range of downstream tasks. The chapter borrows only the many-input, many-output shape.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Scarce Feedback Gate"}
Before a study transitions from proxy evidence to an expensive simulator
or physical-design evaluation, a declared allocation rule should determine
which candidates advance. Routine checks can be automated; the accountable
team defines the rule, reviews exceptions, and decides what claims the resulting
evidence may support. Costlier feedback does not authorize a stronger claim by
itself.
:::

The book uses this prompt as a spine rather than as the only example. Later
chapters zoom into facets of the same request: memory and data movement,
software drift, chiplet partitioning, verification, physical design, and
deployment. Additional examples appear only when a facet needs a more specific
study. Each return keeps the workload, comparison, physical envelope, and
evidence obligations visible so that a changing method is not mistaken for a
changing architectural claim.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Why the Prompt Spans the Stack {#sec-why-the-prompt-spans-the-stack}

The prompt is architectural because each phrase creates obligations beyond the
surface words. A credible study must track workload behavior, software
contracts, hardware organization, physical feasibility, evidence, and rejection
paths together. That obligation stays compact in @tbl-moonshot-decomposition, a
reader's checklist for why the prompt crosses boundaries that architecture
cannot ignore rather than a shopping list of subtasks. The
workload phrase says what behavior the design must serve. The ISA and compute
phrases say what boundary the hardware/software interface must expose. The
power and process phrase says what physical world can reject the idea. The
report phrase says what kind of evidence the study owes the architect before
the answer deserves trust. Each row therefore names both a design decision and
a way for the study to be wrong.

| **Prompt fragment** | **Architectural decisions** | **Evidence or rejection need** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **XRBench mobile XR** | Workload slice, input distribution, quality-of-service target, latency deadline, memory traffic, software pipeline, and drift assumptions. | Trace provenance, benchmark version, documented workload-phase selection, and rejection when results over-optimize a single hot loop or miss real-time behavior. |
| **64-bit RISC-V with vector or accelerator option** | ISA boundary, custom extension policy, programming model, compiler/runtime path, library support, and software compatibility. | Correctness, toolchain support, generated-code evidence, portability checks, and rejection of unsupported software semantics. |
| **Compute subsystem** | CPU, fixed-function accelerator, reconfigurable spatial array (e.g., CGRA or Reconfigurable Dataflow Architecture/RDA), or Processing-in-Memory (PIM) data-centric block; memory hierarchy, SoC integration, interface compatibility, and relevant reliability or isolation assumptions. | Design-space comparison and rejection of candidates that only win by moving cost, bandwidth, energy, complexity, or risk elsewhere. |
| **3 W, 3 nm-class low-power envelope** | Power, voltage/frequency, thermal, area, EDA constraints, and dynamic power management interfaces (e.g., Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling or DVFS, power domains, thermal sensors) for the OS/firmware. | Power-model provenance, physical-design limits (IR drop, electromigration, timing closure), sensitivity analysis, and explicit rejection gates for runtime throttling or reliability failures within the scoped workload. |
| **Design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives** | Alternatives, the Pareto frontier (the objective outcomes of feasible designs for which no alternative is at least as good on every objective and strictly better on at least one) [@Deb2001MultiObjective], assumptions, uncertainty, verification plan, rejected or failed candidates, and accountable decision points. | Connected measurements, assumptions, checks, rejections, coverage, reproducible artifacts, and a named owner for advancing the claim. |

: **Prompt fragments create architecture obligations:** The lighthouse prompt maps to decisions about workload definition, software and ISA contracts, hardware organization, physical feasibility, and evidence. {#tbl-moonshot-decomposition tbl-colwidths="[18,34,35]"}

The table is a diagnostic, not the book's final schema. It lets a reader reject
a weak lighthouse answer immediately when the answer cannot identify its
workload slice, software contract, physical assumptions, supporting
observations, or accountable decision owner. Later chapters give these
obligations precise representations, tool interfaces, evidence practices, and
governance rules. @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card eventually consolidates them
in a compact review form, after the reader has seen why each field is needed.

::: {.callout-engineer-move title="Expose the Hidden Obligations"}
Expand any compact architecture request by answering six questions. For the
lighthouse prompt, @tbl-moonshot-decomposition supplies the first answers.

1. **What architecture question is being formulated?** Name the workload,
   baseline, constraints, scope, and non-goals.
2. **Which meaningful alternatives should be explored?** Separate choices that
   could change the conclusion from trivial parameter variations.
3. **What artifacts and tool path will implement them?** Name the models,
   software, simulators, RTL, or physical-design steps needed for comparison.
4. **Which experiments and counterevidence will evaluate them?** State the
   measurements, fidelity, cost, and failure conditions.
5. **Which architectural mechanisms could explain the result?** Connect the
   measurements to workload behavior, hardware organization, and software.
6. **What recommendation can be defended?** State who owns it, what its limits
   are, and what evidence would reverse it.

A request that cannot answer these questions is not yet a credible
AI-assisted architecture study.
:::

No single model can make these obligations disappear. The table is deliberately
compressed; each row expands into many implementation and evidence questions.
The "3 W, 3 nm-class" row, for example, reaches down into RTL, synthesis,
floorplanning, timing, IR drop, leakage, thermal behavior, and signoff. The
"RISC-V with vector or accelerator option" row reaches sideways into compilers,
runtimes, libraries, generated code, and portability. Architecture development
therefore means proposing artifacts, predicting consequences, optimizing under
constraints, and rejecting weak evidence across changing fidelity levels. This
is why the moonshot is a computer architecture problem rather than prompt
engineering. The study has to connect architectural state across the stack.

## More Than Candidate Generation {#sec-architecture-development-spans-three-roles}

The lighthouse prompt also shows why candidate generation cannot stand for the
whole design process. Different methods may propose an ISA extension,
estimate its latency and energy, search nearby alternatives, critique its
software assumptions, repair an invalid artifact, or verify a result.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization later defines the full set of
method roles and their interactions; these six jobs are the subset this
chapter needs. Here, the narrower point is
that no one kind of automated work can discharge all the obligations in
@tbl-moonshot-decomposition.

The six capabilities describe architecture work. Generation, prediction,
optimization, critique, repair, and verification are bounded jobs that methods
may perform in support of that work. A method job is not a learner outcome, and
no combination of such jobs automatically produces an architecture explanation
or decision.

@fig-architecture-development-triad is a preview, not a taxonomy the reader
must master in this chapter; it draws the three jobs that propose, estimate,
and search, and leaves critique, repair, and verification to the prose that
follows. Its purpose is to show why producing a candidate is only one part of
a credible architecture study.

![**Bounded methods serve a broader architecture study:** Generation proposes artifacts and candidates, prediction estimates behavior and risk, and optimization searches tradeoffs under constraints. These jobs support the six architecture capabilities but do not replace evidence, mechanism-level explanation, or human architectural judgment.](images/F1b-architecture-development-triad){#fig-architecture-development-triad width="100%" fig-alt="Three-circle diagram showing generation, prediction, and optimization as interacting method jobs within a broader AI-assisted architecture study."}

For the mobile XR example, a proposed vector extension remains unsupported
until evaluation estimates its behavior under the power envelope and compares
it with an accelerator, a memory-system change, or a software change. A proxy
may rank candidates one way, a simulator may overturn that ranking, and an EDA
run may return timing paths, congestion, or rule violations rather than a
single score. Search can also overfit[^fn-ml-overfitting] a convenient proxy.
The study must connect these different kinds of work and preserve the reasons a
candidate advanced or failed.

[^fn-ml-overfitting]: Overfitting, in machine learning, is fitting the noise and idiosyncrasies of a training sample so that performance fails to generalize to new data. An optimizer can overfit a proxy the same way, raising the proxy's measured score by exploiting its quirks rather than improving the true objective. The study therefore escalates a promising candidate to stronger evidence before commitment.

The jobs interact, but they do not form a fixed pipeline. Generation can expose
a missing constraint. Prediction can show that the current evidence is too weak
to separate two candidates. Optimization can allocate scarce simulator or EDA
runs, while critique and verification can disqualify the apparent winner.
Repair changes the artifact and may invalidate measurements collected before
the change. The study therefore preserves the consequential state of the work,
including candidates, assumptions, measurements, rejected alternatives, and
the claims those records can support. It does not need every internal model
trace.

In the mobile XR study, generation might produce legal vector configurations,
prediction might estimate their data-movement and power risks, and optimization
might decide which configurations merit expensive evaluation. Those methods
can narrow the work, but they cannot determine whether a compiler assumption is
credible, whether the measured behavior follows from the proposed mechanism,
or whether the remaining tradeoff justifies a commitment. Those are properties
of the broader architecture study.



## Efficiency Claims Need Reviewable Comparisons {#sec-efficiency-claims-need-rejectable-loops}

Exposing the obligations inside the prompt does not yet say what the study is
trying to establish. What the study must establish is an efficiency claim. The
discipline turns scarce resources into useful work through durable
hardware/software interfaces, and the study's product is a credible claim
about that work, not raw artifact output. A design
that is faster but consumes too much power, is impossible to verify, or depends
on fragile software assumptions has not really solved the architectural problem.
If Architecture 2.0 only made architects produce more artifacts, it would not be
enough. The goal is to produce better, more credible, and more efficient systems
under rising complexity.

That gap between producing output and earning a credible commitment is not unique to hardware, and it shows up fastest where generation is already cheap.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Buying Homes Faster Than It Could Say No"}
Zillow ran an automated home-buying business that priced each purchase from a forecasting model and committed real capital on its output. When the estimates drifted from what houses would actually resell for, nothing in the loop was fast or trusted enough to reject the bad buys before the money was spent. The company wound the business down in 2021 after an inventory writedown of roughly three hundred million dollars and cut about a quarter of its staff [@ZillowGroup2021Offers]. Generating an offer was cheap. Trusting one enough to commit was the scarce and ultimately binding step.

**Lesson:** Cheap generation does not by itself set the bottleneck. When admitted
proposals arrive faster than trusted dispositions, the adjudication stage becomes
the bottleneck. If the loop keeps committing without restoring that balance, it
scales its mistakes rather than its wins.
:::

The hard shift is not from performance to a single new metric called power. It
is that efficiency itself is becoming more multidimensional. Classical computer
architecture made performance quantitative, but it also treated cost and power
as first-class constraints
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Dennard-style scaling once
made it easier to improve performance while keeping power density manageable
[@DennardEtAl1974Scaling]. As that story weakened, dark silicon (the share of a chip that must stay powered off to fit its thermal budget) and the limits
of multicore scaling pushed the field toward specialization
[@BorkarChien2011FutureMicroprocessors; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon; @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].
Data-movement energy made arithmetic alone an insufficient efficiency story
[@Horowitz2014Energy]. Warehouse-scale operation expanded the boundary to
power delivery, utilization, networking, operations, and total cost of
ownership [@BarrosoHolzleRanganathan2019DatacenterAsComputer].
Sustainability adds another layer because carbon depends on operational
energy, hardware manufacturing and infrastructure, utilization, geography, and
lifetime
[@GuptaEtAl2021ChasingCarbon].
For an AI-assisted architecture study, each efficiency dimension becomes represented
state, an evidence requirement, and a possible rejection condition.

Given this multidimensional efficiency, the question is not whether traditional architecture methods suddenly stop
working. Many still work extremely well when the workload, abstraction,
feedback path, and commitment level are bounded. The harder question is where
human-orchestrated work becomes too slow, too implicit, or too expensive to
manage the coupled objectives. Architecture 2.0 should make that boundary
explicit, separating the parts that familiar models, scripts, simulation, and
expert review can still handle from the parts that benefit from AI-assisted
formulation, search, implementation, evaluation, explanation, or review.

A credible comparison must also record what failed, not only the winners. That
record keeps later methods, reviewers, and architects from re-exploring ground
the study already ruled out and shows which parts of the design space the claim
actually covers. @sec-data-representations-world-models develops the
representation, and @sec-feedback-verification-trust develops the evidence
practice.

Modern benchmark suites show this same pressure toward multidimensional efficiency. MLPerf, the community benchmark effort for
making machine-learning performance claims reproducible across systems, treats
deployment scenario, latency, throughput, accuracy, and power as part of the
comparison rather than as one scalar claim [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf].
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales develops that case with the numbers; the
architectural lesson here is that efficiency is not one number. It is a
structured claim about useful work under constraints. Useful work must be
evaluated against the relevant scarce resource, such as latency, throughput, energy,
power, area, dollar cost, carbon, reliability, verification effort, engineering
time, or risk.

A compact way to write the point is that every efficiency claim has a design,
workload, scenario, and resource denominator:

$$
\mathrm{Eff}_r(d,w,s) =
\frac{\mathrm{UsefulWork}(d,w,s)}{\mathrm{Resource}_r(d,w,s)}.
$$

Here, $d$ is the design, $w$ is the workload, $s$ is the deployment or
evaluation scenario, and $r$ may be time, energy, power, area, dollar cost,
carbon, validation effort, or another scarce resource. An AI-assisted study
must represent $d$, $w$, $s$, and $r$ explicitly. Changing any one changes what
evidence can support the claim and what should reject it. The equation is
simple on purpose. It prevents a study from calling a design efficient when
the useful work, scenario, or resource denominator has quietly changed. It
also lets a second architect compare competing designs on equal footing rather
than trust whichever number looks larger.

The dimensions this chapter treats as part of efficiency are summarized in
@tbl-efficiency-dimensions. The rows are not separate goals to optimize
independently. They are coupled obligations that a study must represent
and test. Each row pairs the efficiency question a dimension poses with what
complicates an honest comparison of it.

| **Dimension** | **Efficiency question** | **Why it complicates the comparison** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Performance** | How much useful work is delivered per unit time, latency budget, or service-level target? | The answer depends on workload selection, scenario, software stack, and whether the measured behavior matches the deployment claim. |
| **Power and energy** | How much useful work is delivered per watt, joule, thermal budget, or battery envelope? | The study must model activity, data movement, voltage/frequency choices, thermal constraints, and fidelity gaps between estimates and signoff. |
| **Reliability and correctness** | How much useful work survives faults, corner cases, nondeterminism, and validation? | A faster candidate is not efficient if it spends its savings on fragility, debug burden, or invalid software and hardware assumptions. |
| **Scalability and cost** | How much useful work is delivered per dollar, rack, network hop, operator action, or unit of capacity? | Local wins can shift cost to memory, network, power delivery, utilization, operations, or total cost of ownership. |
| **Sustainability** | How much useful work is delivered per unit of operational and embodied environmental footprint? | Carbon depends on hardware lifetime, manufacturing, energy mix, utilization, and where and when computation runs. |
| **Evidence and engineering effort** | How much credible evidence is obtained per simulation, experiment, verification run, or engineer-hour? | A study that generates more candidates can still be inefficient if it consumes scarce feedback, hides failures, or produces evidence that cannot reject outputs. |

: **Efficiency is becoming multidimensional:** Performance, power, reliability, scalability, sustainability, and evidence cost are increasingly coupled. The architecture question is how a study compares them without hiding shifted cost or changed scope. {#tbl-efficiency-dimensions tbl-colwidths="[22,32,34]"}

The word efficient should therefore be read broadly. A candidate that improves
simulated performance while increasing verification burden may not be
efficient. A candidate that reduces energy but requires fragile software
assumptions may not be efficient. A candidate that looks good under a proxy
metric but fails under a more faithful workload may not be efficient.
Architecture 2.0 should treat efficiency as a scoped architecture claim, not a
property inferred from one attractive artifact or score.

The lighthouse prompt makes this concrete. The requested subsystem must
support a workload class, meet a power envelope, fit a technology assumption,
interact with software, and produce evidence. The study must compare
tradeoffs among energy, latency, memory traffic, programmability,
verification, and deployment risk. A single scalar objective may be useful
inside the study, but it cannot be the whole architectural judgment.

## Boundaries of the Argument

With credible efficiency claims set as the target, the boundaries deserve
equal clarity. This book is not a paper catalog. The field is moving too quickly
for a catalog to remain useful for long. The goal is to give readers a
framework that can organize current work and still be useful as models, tools,
and benchmarks change.

Nor is the goal to make a product forecast. The book does not claim that a
particular model, tool harness, simulator, EDA flow, or benchmark will define
the field. Those will evolve. The durable questions are which architecture
decision the work advances, how AI contributes, and what evidence and limits
make the claim defensible.

Nor is this a tool manual. Tools matter deeply, but the focus is how they
support architecture questions, artifacts, experiments, explanations, and
decisions rather than installation instructions or process recipes.
@sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping gives a compact bootstrap path.
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives a compact review form and rubric.

It is also not a claim that AI systems replace architects. As AI participation
widens, architectural responsibility changes rather than disappears. An
architect or accountable engineering team must frame the task, choose
abstractions, representations, tools, and fidelity, interpret mechanisms, set
evidence standards, define what may be automated, and remain answerable for
consequential claims. @sec-data-representations-world-models develops the
records that make such work inspectable.

::: {.callout-failure-mode title="What Not to Claim"}
Architecture 2.0 should not be read as push-button chip design, replacement of
architects, or a claim that today's generative methods define the field. The
defensible claim is broader than one method and more bounded than autonomy.
Appropriate AI assistance, grounded in architecture representations, tools,
and experiments, can improve how architects formulate problems, explore and
implement alternatives, evaluate and explain results, and defend decisions.
:::

The rest of the book develops that discipline. @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales
diagnoses where rising specialization and system coupling create useful roles
for AI and where they merely move the bottleneck. @sec-architecture-20-ontology
defines the bounded study, claim, loop, and supporting instruments.
@sec-data-representations-world-models and
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces develop the representations,
data, environments, and tool interfaces needed for implementation.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization separates the bounded jobs of
generation, prediction, and optimization, and
@sec-feedback-verification-trust develops evaluation and evidence.
@sec-running-the-loop constructs and analyzes the lighthouse study without
turning that chapter into a lab. @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack tests transfer
across architecture layers, and @sec-what-architect-owns integrates the six
capabilities into a defensible recommendation. The appendices supply practical
review artifacts and a resource catalog.

## Open Research Questions

These open research questions set the field-level agenda for the rest of the
book. They are intentionally broad because this chapter is the entry point. The
later chapters make state, environments, methods, evidence, execution, and
ownership more concrete.

1. **When is AI-assisted design work an architecture contribution?**
AI-assisted work becomes architectural when it changes what can be credibly
understood, built, compared, or decided under architectural constraints. A
research rubric here would distinguish architectural contributions in
artifacts, methods, datasets, environments, findings, and decisions from
novelty in the AI method alone. A strong evaluation would test reviewer agreement on borderline papers
and show where the rubric changes review outcomes. This question uses the
different modes of participation in @sec-from-architecture-10-to-architecture-20.

2. **How should venues review AI-assisted architecture studies?**
Architecture venues already know how to inspect artifacts, baselines, and
quantitative tradeoffs. AI-assisted studies add represented state, permitted
actions, method roles, rejected alternatives, and commitment boundaries to the
evidence behind an architectural claim.
A paper or community study could propose review criteria, artifact-review
norms, and call-for-papers language for this new class of work, then test
whether reviewers identify unsupported commitments earlier. The moonshot
framing makes this a community problem, not a matter of
individual paper style.

3. **When do rejected alternatives count as architecture evidence?**
Rejected candidates can define a design-space boundary, expose invalid
assumptions, and give later studies useful negative data. A reporting standard for
rejected alternatives should record what was tried, what evidence rejected it,
what claim the rejection supports, and when the rejection expires. The research
question is whether reviewers can use that record to reconstruct the boundary
of a claim without treating weak evidence as final truth. The stack-spanning
prompt in @sec-why-the-prompt-spans-the-stack and the efficiency claims in @sec-efficiency-claims-need-rejectable-loops show where this record would
matter.

4. **What shared infrastructure makes progress comparable?**
If AI-assisted architecture remains a collection of isolated demonstrations,
the field will struggle to compare results. Shared problem and case packets,
datasets, workload suites, environments, baselines, experiment protocols,
explanation criteria, and instructor-ready studies would let groups produce
contrastable findings without pretending every architecture problem has one
fixed benchmark. The lighthouse decomposition in
@tbl-moonshot-decomposition and the MLPerf discussion in
@sec-efficiency-claims-need-rejectable-loops provide starting examples.

5. **What should an architecture moonshot leave behind?**
A field-shaping moonshot should leave more than one impressive design. It
should leave tasks, instruments, baselines, evidence standards, trained
reviewers, and maintained artifacts that make later work easier to trust. A
research program could evaluate past architecture and AI-infrastructure
examples by asking which artifacts survived the flagship result and which ones
enabled follow-on work, negative results, or fair rejection. This is the
field-level lesson of the moonshot examples and the AlphaChip war story.

## Conclusion

This chapter asked what it would take for AI to help an architect formulate,
explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend a system design rather than
settle for a plausible candidate. The lighthouse turns a compact prompt into a
bounded architecture study with a question, meaningful alternatives,
checkable artifacts, experiments, a mechanism-level explanation, and a
recommendation. The study is the unit of work, and its architectural claim is
the unit another architect reviews.

That shift is forced by efficiency, now read as a coupled budget across
performance, energy, power delivery, reliability, sustainability, cost,
verification burden, and time to credible evidence, rather than a single number.
When plausible artifacts are cheap, the scarce work is the burden of proof, and
the binding constraint may shift among search, tool feedback, interpretation,
evidence, and decision. A represented design loop helps execute and inspect
that work. It is one mechanism within the discipline the book names.

None of this removes the architect, nor does it require a person to approve
every routine check. The architect or accountable engineering team frames the
task, chooses abstractions, representations, tools, and experiments, explains
the architectural mechanisms, sets evidence standards, declares what may be
automated, and remains responsible for the resulting claim. Architecture 2.0
uses AI to improve architecture work while keeping the path from question to
decision explicit enough for others to challenge and improve.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Read any AI architecture result as a bounded study. Identify its question, alternatives, artifacts, experiments, mechanism-level explanation, recommendation, and evidence limits.
- **Reader test:** Can you reconstruct how AI contributed across those obligations well enough to challenge the architecture claim and its reversal conditions?
- **Up next:** The next chapter asks where AI genuinely improves that work and where it merely moves the bottleneck.
:::
